data.title: British American Tobacco: The Case For Its Smokeless Future
data.description:
Introduction

British American Tobacco (BTI) is the last remaining giant of the tobacco industry that I have to review. With its current strategy of "A Better Tomorrow" and the current dynamics of the tobacco industry, I want to see how BTI fares against its rivals, Philip Morris and Altria, and see if it is well-positioned to capture the future of the tobacco industry.

BTI currently has a wide portfolio of brands in both new categories, such as nicotine pouches with VELO, the up-and-coming rival of ZYN, as well as traditional cigarettes with well-known brands such as Kent and Lucky Strike.

Current Dynamics & Segment Overview

BTI, being based in the UK, does not release quarterly earnings; therefore, I’ll base what has been going on in the business on the recent outlook update. BTI is now expecting a 2% growth in revenue and overall operating profit, which has been the relative trend for the tobacco industry in general, with many looking at the growth segment to offset the structural decline in cigarette brands. Going deeper, current CEO Tadeu Marroco stated that Vuse and the nicotine pouch segment have seen volume and revenue improvements in the US, which is encouraging, though the Vapour category continues to be impacted by illicit proliferation. All of this has pushed BTI to expand its share buyback program to £1.3B for FY2026. Moreover, the company has maintained its operating cash flow conversion rate of over 95%, which highlights how the company has championed highly disciplined capital management.

Now I want to go segment by segment so that investors can grasp the current state of BTI’s operation. The current star of the portfolio in my mind is akin to ZYN for PM, VELO, which has driven the surge in the company's stock price. Currently, the VELO Plus line has been gaining high traction in the US, seeing triple-digit revenue growth in the second half of 2025, and it has been gaining market share at a rapid pace in the nicotine pouch industry. This line is using synthetic nicotine instead of nicotine derived from tobacco plants; thus, it offers a cleaner chemical profile than other products on shelves. The experience is apparently also better with its wet pouch design and instant release of nicotine effects, which should gain traction among users (I’m certainly not pushing the use of nicotine products, but I’m just stating how BTI could gain an advantage over competitors). The overall segment has reached full-year profitability in 2025, which is far ahead of what many analysts were expecting.

On the e-cigarette front, or the Vapour segment, it includes the Vuse product lines, which have seen a roller coaster in terms of revenue for the year as the industry is fighting against illegal disposable vapes that have been a drag against legal and FDA-approved brands like Vuse. The FDA has begun to act majorly against these, which is why the segment has recovered with its dominant market share in the US. In a similar segment, there are also heated products with brands like glo and neo that have seen relatively flat performance as BTI is facing major competition from PM’s IQOS, mainly in Japan. To counteract this, BTI is focusing on the rollout of glo Hilo, which would be a premium offering for BTI. I do not expect this segment to be a major driver of earnings for the company, though I understand that it needs to have a presence in the market to fight off PM.

And now on to the legacy core with traditional cigarettes that include many in-house brands, as previously mentioned, as well as the rights to Camel and Newport in the US. The industry has been grappling with lower volumes that are set to decline by around 2% annually for the whole industry, yet BTI and its peers have been successfully using their pricing power, thanks to the premium perception of their brands, to maintain actual revenue growth. Looking at the US, BTI is actually seeing a rebound, with the segment returning to revenue and profit growth for the year, thanks to its constant market share. Shining brands in the portfolio include Newport, with its dominant position in the menthol category despite having ongoing regulatory threats. Others would be Lucky Strike and Natural American Spirit, which have also been stars in 2025 as they have been gaining market share thanks to their appeal to different consumers who are more value-seekers.

BTI has gone through a major accounting shift to amortize the value of the company’s US cigarette brands, giving most of them a 30-year life span, acknowledging that these will eventually have zero value in the future, and aligning the company with its motto of being smokeless by 2035. Mainly, this segment acts as the big cash cow thanks to its immense FCF generation that is able to fund the expansion into smokeless products as well as strengthen the balance sheet and return excesses to shareholders.

Valuation

At first, BTI looks relatively expensive with its P/E standing at nearly 30, but it is important to note that this is mainly due to impairment charges. Right now, BTI trades at a forward P/E of 12.54, which is much more in line with the industry. Regarding FCF, it has also turned negative due to a “once-in-a-generation” legal event that was the Canadian Tobacco Litigation Settlement. The Ontario Superior Court approved a C$32.5B settlement in which the companies were required to pay out nearly all of their cash on hand upfront. Furthermore, there will be an 85% profit sweep going directly to settlement payments for years to come in Canada. By taking an adjusted look at FCF, overlooking the settlement, BTI is expected to generate £7.04B for 2025 and has been trading at around a 15% FCF yield over the past 7 years. Another argument would be its generous dividend yield, currently standing at 5.4%, which allows investors to be patient with BTI.

Growth for the company is now inadvertently tied to the smokeless transformation, which is a good thing. The company is now entering a quality growth phase where it expects significant acceleration of its financial performance starting in 2026. Both the regulatory cleanup for Vuse and the launch of VELO Plus should be a great tailwind, as well as cigarettes being able to offset declining volumes. This has been factored into the stock already, with its peer-leading 55.45% YTD return, the largest among its peers, but I still see some upside as BTI realizes the upside of its strategy. Looking ahead, I want to see how VELO fares against ZYN and if it can continue to take market share away from the dominant competitor in the space.

BTI currently has some of the best margins in the industry, potentially because it was further behind in terms of smokeless products compared to Philip Morris, which tends to have lower margins than cigarettes. With the smokeless product range taking on a larger share of revenue, the company should see its overall profitability dip, although I’d rather have a sustainable business with lower margins than a dying one with high margins. The transition phase means that BTI can use the high level of cash flows from cigarettes to finance its growth products. Mainly, Nicotine pouches will certainly have the best margin prospects in my opinion, as they are simple dry goods that don’t require Batteries or complex electronics like vapour & heated tobacco. Furthermore, with the company’s plan to save around £2B by 2030 through supply chain automation and office streamlining, we could still see incremental margin growth.

Risks

The most imminent risk to investing in BTI would be the structural decline of cigarettes, which are still a core revenue driver. Right now, BTI can offset the volume decline with price hikes, but soon there will be a tipping point when consumers will accelerate the trend to go towards cheaper alternatives, stop smoking, or, most favorably for BTI, go towards smokeless options.

Litigation is the other main issue, as highlighted by the most recent Canadian lawsuits. The operations in the country will already be almost non-profitable, with 85% of Canadian net income swept away. It is a specific case, but it sets a precedent for many countries that could see massive class-action settlements.

Conclusion

BTI’s future is now heavily tilted towards smokeless alternatives, which is a relatively good thing. Margins will be slightly compressed, but the company has the dominant position in e-cigarettes and is rapidly catching up to Zyn in the nicotine pouches area. With its high dividend yield and the narrative already switching to the potential of its smokeless products, I believe that British American Tobacco is a Buy.

data.title: AXIA Energia: My Bet For Brazil 2026
data.description:
AXIA Energia (AXIA), formerly called Electrobras, is, I think, a great long-term investment opportunity in the Brazilian energy sector. If you're looking for Latin American assets related to the energy potential of the region, I believe AXIA is the best option, both for its national and continental weight. With an installed capacity of 44.4 GW, it represents 18% of the country's total installed capacity of 251 GW.

No other company in the region comes close to such installed capacity. Among them we can mention Itaipu Binacional, with an installed capacity of 14 GW, or the mega hydroelectric dam, Three Gorges Dam, built by China and with an installed capacity of 22 GW. We can also mention Enel Americas, with 16 GW of installed capacity for Latin America. In Argentina, for example, the company with the most installed capacity is Pampa Energía (PAM) with 5.4 GW. So, with this quick brief, you can see there's no other company with an installed capacity of 44.4 GW as AXIA.

Besides, Brazil attracts me for the size of its market and its demographics, with more than 213 million inhabitants, and because it represents 57.6% of South America's installed capacity. In other words: whatever happens to AXIA will be reflected in Brazil in energy terms, and vice versa.

That's why I like this company so much, because it is in the middle of the Brazilian energy transition, with 100% of its energy coming from renewable sources after the sale of Santa Cruz, the company's last gas plant (see slide 5 of Investor Presentation). But there's something even better: the company is not only advancing in the sustainable energy transition but also in the transition from regulated prices to free prices, within the framework of operational efficiency that began in 2022 with its privatization.

Free price, a key catalyst

The transition from regulated prices to free prices is, in my view, the central point of the operational improvements I see in the company and the main catalyst for revenue growth. I'll tell you why. If you take a look at the following image, you'll see the considerable improvement from the sale of energy at R$170/MWh in Q3 2024 and the sale of energy at R$252/MWh in Q3 2025.

This also improved the unit margin, from R$55/MWh to R$95/MWh, and therefore also the total contribution. The price improvements even offset a drop in power generation in Q3, recovering slightly to 69% in Q4. If prices had remained regulated, this would have been a problem for the company, but on the contrary, with the current situation, AXIA is improving its margins, and it still can pay more dividends on the quarter, as I'll show later.

But the catalyst I like the most related to energy price increases is in the future: Fitch Ratings estimates energy prices of R$252/MWh in 2026 and R$217/MWh in 2027, with up to 8 GW of energy available for new contracts in 2028. With prices above R$252/MWh, I estimate margins have a floor of R$95/MWh, as is happening now. And I talk about a "floor" because as soon as the volume of energy generated increases towards the levels of early 2025, that will imply fewer purchases and greater profitability.

But the transition to free prices isn't the only catalyst that's calling my attention. Part of its efficiency strategy is focused on the sale of heavy assets to lower leverage and costs, a factor that Fitch Ratings also added to its analysis. One of those assets in the sales process is Electronuclear, for R$535 million, which I add to the Santa Cruz gas-fired power plant and the shareholding in EMAE as part of asset recycling. In the same process, I think the goal is to not neglect leadership in generation, transmission, and distribution, and that is why they bought the Tijoá Energia power plant for R$247 million (slides 14 and 15 of the Investor Presentation).

As I mentioned before, this asset recycling is also part of the objectives to lower leverage from 1.9x LTM, with net debt of R$42.7 billion (total debt: R$72 billion). This debt is a little high, but I think it's part of the company's transition process, which went from state control to a private structure, and it's gaining interesting catalysts. I don't see the debt maturity schedule as complicated; in fact, the highest maturity is in 2026, R$9.7 billion, so I could cover part of that debt with free cash flow.

The other point I'm watching very closely is that the majority of the debt is at indexed rates, which is logical in Brazil, where inflation is high (although it is falling) and the interest rates of the Central Bank of Brazil are also usually high (today they are at 15%), even well above inflation.

33.8% of AXIA's debt is indexed to inflation, with the addition of a 5.97% spread. This seems to be a lot, but we must take into account that Brazilian energy prices in the free market are also adjusting to the inflation trends, or even above them. This is what keeps me calm.

The other difficult part of the debt is the one that is indexed to the CDI, which usually replicates the Central Bank's interest rate and represents 59.6% of all debt. CDI is a variable rate that could cause some uncertainty in the long term if Brazil's macroeconomics deteriorate. However, I believe the maturity schedule to be key in this scenario: I believe the best possible time to renegotiate the debt is to wait for a lower interest rate environment. Given its long maturity horizon, I believe this works in its favor.

Looking for efficiency

Efficiency is my main interest in the company because it is not achieving immediate liquidity. In Q3, free cash flow was R$4.9 billion, but between dividend payments, debt repayments, and litigation expenses, net free cash was negative by R$1.4 billion.

I didn't find the dividend distribution to be wrong. I do agree with the idea that the company must try harder to compensate the shareholders. I simply would like to see a lower proportion between the dividend payments and the free cash flow to achieve a more solid cash position. I looked up the Q1 and Q2 results and found that the Dividends/FCF ratio was 53% and 71%, respectively. In Q3 it was 81%. Goldman Sachs asked a particular question about this to the directors during the call: if the dividend payment methodology included future asset sales.

Bruno Amorim Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division

What extent that leverage includes as a factor to reduce net debt? Why am I asking this question? One of the reasons you gave us to pay out this dividend was the sale of some assets. So I want to understand the rationale behind it. When you announce the dividend, are you considering the assets that are available for sale will be sold? Or as they are sold, you can trigger more dividends to pay out?

Ivan de Souza Monteiro President, CEO & President of Executive Board

We do not take into account assets that haven't been traded yet. That isn't the rationale -- that's not the rationale of the methodology. As to capital allocation, you're right. As the company starts -- well, we always had the impression we're lagging behind. Now we're more familiar with the risks that are inherent to the company and preparing the company to live with that risk more proactively with more alternatives, financially, operational solutions. Bottom line is that once we know that what the cash flow will be in the future, you have more room to allocate capital.

I really liked the answer, and it shows they are not being conservative in the search for efficiency. I do believe they are trying to compensate the shareholders, and I think this is something really good. But I must repeat: I'd like to see more efficiency to avoid debt problems in the future.

Catalysts and investments in Q3 results

The efforts to achieve efficiency that I've mentioned before can also be seen in the Q3 results. PMSO expenses (personnel, materials, services, and other) were $1.6 billion in the quarter compared to R$2 billion in Q3 2024. Besides, another efficiency example that called my attention was the R$6.4 billion EBITDA, an increase YoY of 3.4% driven by the increase in transmission revenue. This offset the 2.1% drop in generation revenue. In the EBITDA of R$6.4 billion, the company excluded the results of the Amazonas thermal power plants sold in May 2025 (source: slide 10 of Q3 results).

The company had revenue of R$10 billion and a net profit of R$2.1 billion, a drop of 68% YoY, but mostly explained by the normalization of previously regulated prices. In other words, during 2024, especially in Q3 2024, net profit grew for the only time due to the non-recurring regulatory effects of the Periodic Tariff Review. That's why I think it's very important to show you the EBITDA first, which clears up these accounting noises and shows a YoY increase of 3.4%.

The results presentation also revealed some catalysts for future investment that I liked in the long term. For example, the company won the ANEEL auction for four lots for the construction and maintenance of transmission lines, with a RAP of R$138 million and a CAPEX of R$1.6 billion. There will be another energy auction in March 2026, and the company wants to participate in another battery auction in the second quarter of 2026.

In that set of catalysts, investments are part of the efficiency strategy, in my opinion. CAPEX grew 57% in Q3, reaching R$2.7 billion, driven by improvements in the transmission segment. In this segment, the company has 230 projects with a projected CAPEX of R$12.5 billion until 2030, which will represent additional income of a RAP of R$1.7 billion. The company could also supply a new data center in São Paulo. The investment outlook is also a factor I like about the company as it continues to improve operational efficiency.

Valuation

I do like the company's growth projections for the future in the context of the mentioned catalysts: from the sale of energy at better prices to the high demand for expected consumption. If the company continues to successfully execute its transition to optimal efficiency, with strong dividend payouts as in 2025, I believe it could be worth much more than its current price. Even more: I really think it's worth it, but the market hasn't yet discounted that potential, in part because, I believe, of the political risks in Brazil in 2026, which I'll talk about later.

I tried to explain at the beginning that I see AXIA as the biggest company in South America in its sector because it has the largest installed capacity in GW and is located in the largest country in the region. However, I feel that the stock price doesn't yet reflect that. The EV/EBITDA FWD of 8.9x is below the industry median of 11.7x, while the EV/EBIT FWD is even lower than the industry: 12x versus 19.5x. I also believe the current price doesn't correctly reflect the Price-to-Book, with a difference versus the sector of 32.2%.

Furthermore, when I looked for other large companies in the sector in the region, even those with lower capitalization than AXIA, I found that they have a higher EV/EBITDA. Pampa Energia has a 9.8x ratio, Enel Chile (ENIC) 13x, and Companhia Paranaense de Energia (ELP) 10.2x.

Risks 2026

The process of recycling assets to alleviate the debt burden was a topic I already addressed. But I must go back to it because debt is evidently a risk for the company. I consider another important risk to be that the interest rates of the Central Bank of Brazil remain at the current levels of 15% for a long time or, in the worst case, that they rise even further. In November, the YoY inflation came in below 5%, a downward trend that I hope will continue so that interest rates will also keep pace.

However, we must add to the effect of interest rates, there is the election year of 2026, where political uncertainty usually affects the markets. I should also mention the probable re-election of Lula da Silva and his hypothetical fourth term that could generate some instability, mostly because his previous government was a very strict regulator of energy prices, and returning to that regime would pose a serious risk to the successful operational improvement process that AXIA is undertaking. Maybe the current Lula will not go back to that regime, but he's not acting that way now.

Therefore, I want to make it clear that I am not saying that he will return to that regime or that he will reverse privatizations in the case of a fourth term. I'm just saying investors might think this way, and therefore, they might sell their positions in Brazil. It's just the usual climate of uncertainty in a Latin American election year. Once the storm has passed, I believe AXIA will continue with its strong fundamentals in the long term. I think some of those risks are implicit in the current price, and that's why I believe it's a very good buying option.

data.title: Entergy: Favorable Demand And Regulatory Trends (Upgrade)
data.description:
While shares of Entergy (ETR) have been a strong performer over the past year, the stock has pulled back about 5% from highs, which has coincided with increasing worries about AI data center buildouts and their impact on electricity prices. This is of particular importance for Entergy, given its large capital program and significant planned data center construction in its territories. I last covered shares in August, rating the stock a hold, which has been the right call, as its 3% rally has lagged behind the market’s 9% rise. With updated financials and increased focus on utility pricing power as we head into 2026, now is a good time to revisit ETR.

Rising electricity prices are a growing political concern

Looking first at the macro backdrop, last Thursday, capacity prices on PJM, the nation’s largest grid, hit record levels, speaking to the pressure incremental demand from data centers and other growth is placing on the electrical grid. While Entergy is not tied to the PJM grid, this speaks to the broader pressure facing the utility industry. Capacity prices are up about 1,000% over the past two years. Now, this is just a component of energy bills, but capacity prices are a portion of utility bills paid to generators to supply energy when power demand spikes. Still, this increase will contribute to further utility bill increases, likely to exacerbate anger over electricity pressures. Over time, this could lead to pushback from regulators on accepting rate increases by utilities.

As you can see below, electricity prices have been surging after being stable for most of the 2010s. General cost inflation and higher natural gas prices have played a role, but data center demand is becoming an increasing focus. Frankly, we are still in the early innings of the data center story—most of the facilities are merely being planned or constructed. There will be substantially more data center demand in 5 years than today (even if bullish projections are undershot). As such, to the extent that data centers cause electricity price inflation, the future impact is likely to be more significant than the impact to this point.

While I am not making a political statement (that is separate from investing), it is true that the political climate matters for utility stocks. Utilities receive regulatory-approved rates of return, and utility commissioners are generally chosen by elected officials. To the extent there is rising anger over higher utility prices, there may be efforts to make it harder for utilities to earn the same return or recoup investments. Conversely, I could foresee efforts to make large tech firms pay for incremental buildout costs to support data centers rather than spreading that cost across existing customers. All else equal, utility recapture is likely to face more scrutiny over the next five years than the past five years nationally, which is a reason utility shares have faced some pressure in my view.

Entergy bills are fairly low

Given this political pressure, utility bill costs are essential to track. Within this framework, I view ETR as well-positioned. As you can see below, its customers have much lower rates than the national average, and their annual inflation has also been consistently lower. While not immune to political pressure, this is a reason I see ETR as poised to securely recover its cap-ex, especially given pro-development activities in Arkansas and Louisiana. Importantly, companies like Alphabet (GOOG) have committed to funding the cost of powering new facilities. This reduces the political blowback associated with the buildout.

Importantly, aside from AI, I view Entergy as having one of the strongest industrial growth profiles. Hyundai has opened a significant facility, and demand should continue to ramp up. Louisiana is also the center of the US LNG export industry, which is energy-intensive and quickly growing. I expect ongoing growth in US energy exports, which should provide further demand tailwinds for ETR, justifying its capital program. In fact, as you can see, industrial demand at ETR has far outpaced national industrial production. Given energy exports, I expect this superior growth profile to persist.

Cap-ex plans are large and growing

Still, data centers are a critical tailwind. The company sees 7-12 gigawatts of data center demand, up from 5-10 previously. Meta (META) is leading the way with a massive planned facility in Louisiana. Like many utilities, Entergy is in the midst of a large-scale multiyear capital program to both modernize its grid and increase supply. Given growing demand, it sees 19 GWs of needed capacity. Management now expects to spend $41 billion, up $1 billion from last quarter. In November, Entergy signed a 20-year natural gas supply deal with Energy Transfer (ET), which will help provide it with fuel to support growth on its grid from the Meta data center and ongoing industrial development.

About $4.4 billion of this will be funded with equity issuance. It has already funded 45% of this equity issuance via forward sales, and I do not expect any further dilution over the next 18 months beyond existing forward sales. Due to equity issuance, there has been a 5% increase in the share count over the past year. I expect any incremental cap-ex to be funded by at least 20% of added equity.

The capital program will primarily be funded by operating cash flow, and ETR anticipates borrowing ~$18 billion. ETR has ample borrowing capacity even with $29 billion of debt. FFO/debt is above 16%, and the company aims to keep this above 14%. I expect some leveraging in 2026-2027 as it invests before recapturing the cost, and then as EBITDA grows, leverage should come back down.

Growth targets are credible

We are already seeing the benefits of increased activity on its grid and recapture of past cap-ex spending. In the company’s third quarter, reported back on October 29th, Entergy earned $1.53, which beat estimates by $0.09. Alongside results, ETR narrowed guidance to $3.85-$3.95 of EPS this year, raising the lower end by $0.10 from prior guidance. Adjusting for weather, volumes were up 4.5%, reflecting the strong industrial growth in its territories. Industrial activity was up 7%. Thanks to rising earnings, in November, Entergy increased its dividend by 7% to $0.64, and shares currently offer a 2.8% dividend yield.

Alongside its capital program, management is targeting 8+% EPS growth through 2029. I view Entergy as having one of the most credible EPS trajectories. It has locked in most of its equity needs, reducing dilution risk. Its growth is more diversified thanks to clear industrial tailwinds from energy exports. It also has a favorable regulatory environment, and its low customer bills enable easier rate recapture. Plus, its balance sheet is less levered than needed at its desired credit ratings. As such, I expect ETR to deliver at the upper end of guidance, and an additional data center would support incremental upside.

Shares are a buy

Back in August, I was targeting ~$112 by 2028 and about $120 in 2029, given its growth profile. This points to ~6.5-7% long-term appreciation. Combined with its dividend, I see about a ~9.5% total return opportunity over the next four years. This leaves ETR right at the cusp of my ~10% buy target. After a period of consolidation, I am indeed moving ETR to a “buy.” In a period of increasing macro uncertainty, I view ETR’s predictable, regulated cash flows as increasingly attractive, especially with long-term rates unlikely to rise in my view.

While I do not expect 20+% annual returns to continue, I see ETR as offering an attractive mix of income and growth. Plus, its cost profile leaves it among the best-positioned utilities for a harsher political climate, an increasingly important consideration. Combined with favorable industrial growth, ETR is a best-in-class utility, and I would add shares here.
